questions.prompt: Your eval suite has 40 cases and they all pass at 100%. What does that mean?

choices.text: Your eval is probably too easy. Healthy evals live in the 70–90% range. A 100% suite has stopped catching the things you ship to improve. Add harder cases, sample real production failures, and rotate easy cases out.
Switch models — the current one is overqualified.
Stop running the eval; it's a waste of tokens.

questions.explanation: An eval at 100% has hit ceiling and can no longer measure improvements or regressions. It's a sign you've trained the prompt against those cases or the model has gotten too good for them. Add adversarial, edge, and regression cases sourced from real production failures.

questions.prompt: You want to grade whether a support bot's response is helpful and accurate. The right answer isn't a fixed string — tone, completeness, and faithfulness matter. What grading approach fits?

choices.text: Model-based grading (LLM-as-judge) with a structured rubric returning JSON. Use a strong judge model, calibrate it against a sample of human ratings first, and add explicit rubric items so the judge doesn't just prefer longer answers.
Have a human grade every single output forever.

questions.explanation: Subjective qualities like helpfulness and tone are exactly where LLM-as-judge shines. The catches: use a capable judge model, write a clear rubric, and calibrate against ~30 human-graded examples so you know the judge agrees with humans. Once calibrated, the judge scales cheaply.
